body:
Purpose: To introduce the proposed requirements in the Works and Services Bylaw (replacement of the Subdivision and Development Control Bylaw). Staff presentation by Carl Isaak, Director Neighborhood Planning and Urban Design, and Nima Najafi, Director Engineering Development

Project Overview:
Works and Services Bylaw1
Project Overview: Works and Services Bylaw
Background1
Bill 44 (2023)
Required all single -detached zones to 
be rezoned to SSMUH zoning.
•Resulting in the rezoned of 30,000+ 
lots from single detached and 
duplex lots to SSMUH, 
•Allowing up to 4 units (and 6 units 
near transit) on SSMUH lots
•no further rezoning needed, just 
building permit
•current and previously planned 
infrastructure not sufficient to 
support expanded density the R1 
zone

Project Overview: Works and Services Bylaw
Background1
Bill 16 (2024)
Expanded list of items can be required for 
frontage upgrades , such as:
•street furniture, parklets, streetlamps and 
sustainable design features [e.g. rain gardens]
Expanded road dedication authority to require to 
provide more land for sustainable design features 
and alternative modes of transportation, at time 
of building permit/subdivision, such as: 
•wider sidewalks, bike lanes, street trees and 
traffic calming.

Project Overview: Works and Services Bylaw
Background1
Bill 16 – Housing Statutes Amendment Act, 2024 opportunities
1.This expanded authority allows formalization of frontage improvement 
requirements 
2.Most streetscape improvements envisioned in current City policies can 
now be required by bylaw (previously secured through rezoning) 
3.Doing so will foster more predictable, consistent infrastructure delivery 
for all developments (including those without rezoning)
4.For the development industry this increases transparency, efficiency, 
and predictability
Project Overview: Works and Services Bylaw
Background1
Bill 16 (2023)
May 15, 2024 - Interim amendment to the 
Subdivision Control Bylaw 1971 address:
1. servicing requirements as part of 
Building Permit issuance added
» to implement authority to require 
works and services at BP issuance, 
rather than just at subdivision
» SD Control Bylaw standards previously 
informed servicing requirements at 
rezoning, but were not the authority to 
require works and services
2. staff report noted future initiative to 
replace the current Subdivision 
Control Bylaw 1971 with a new bylaw.

Key Changes & New Regulations
Extended Services and Latecomer Agreements 2
It is proposed that the following authorities are delegated to the 
General Manager 
•to require extended or excess works and services (which serve adjacent 
properties as well), and
•enter into latecomers agreements
The delegation will allow the GM Engineering to:
•require an owner to provide extended or excess works and services
•determine the proportion that serve other properties
•identify the other benefitting properties
•establish and impose a latecomer charge
Latecomer policy and procedures will be developed to support this 
delegated authority.
Key Changes & New Regulations
Highway/Road and Related Infrastructure Widths 
and Standards2
Table 2 in Schedule A
•Identifies the required right of 
way (ROW) widths
•To accommodate trees on all 
streets and green infrastructure 
features:
1.Additional road width proposed 
outside of the Town Centre on 
arterial and collector roads 
2.Up to 1m compared to the 
Transportation Plan on streets 
with cycle facilities
2 lane Collector with 
Cycle track 4 lane Arterial with 
Cycle track 
2
Schedule C 
• Supplements Table 2 (required ROW 
widths) by providing a refined 
overview of road classifications
• Identifies future roads to be provided 
based on Transportation Plan and OCP 
with some limited refinement
• Identifies pathways where anticipated 
in the OCP , to break up large blocks 
and provide convenient, direct 
pedestrian and cycling routes.
Schedule CKey Changes & New Regulations
Highway/Road and Related Infrastructure Widths 
and Standards
Key Changes & New Regulations
Highway/Road and Related Infrastructure Widths 
and Standards2
Schedule D
Identifies Arterial and Collector 
roads that need to accommodate 
cycling facilities or rapid transit
•Additional road width required 
to accommodate the 
infrastructure
•Specific cross -section specified 
in the Design Criteria Manual 
(DCM)
Schedule D
Key Changes & New Regulations
Mid-block Connection Policy2
Mid-block connections, to improve 
permeability through longer blocks in 
higher density areas, are currently 
negotiated on a case -by-case bases 
through rezonings
Separate Policy (not in bylaw) 
proposed for mid block pedestrian 
and cycling connections. This 
standardizes the requirements to 
provide a midblock connection to 
increase p redictability and consistency

Key Changes & New Regulations
Migration of Subdivision Authority2
The current Subdivision and Development Control Bylaw 1971 contains 
delegated subdivision authorities to the approving officer to approval 
subdivisions. 
These delegations will now be covered in Zoning Bylaw 2026 and the 
Development Procedures Bylaw 2026. 
The proposed Works and Services Bylaw 2026 will only cover the required 
frontage upgrades associated with development.
Key Changes & New Regulations
Process Improvement2
Streamlining developmental approvals is a key goal of the new bylaw. 
»developers can anticipate dedication needs and frontage 
upgrade costs early in the process.
»standardized expectations
»reduce uncertainty
»enabling consultants to prepare accurate submission packages, 
and
»supporting faster staff review of required works and services.
Key Changes & New Regulations
Fees and Charges2
Currently, Engineering review fees are not collected until a Servicing 
Agreement is signed.
Staff time is spent and no fees collected if developer delayed or abandoned 
projects. 
New initial application fee under W&S Bylaw is to account for upfront work 
by City staff and will be deducted from the final fee payable when Servicing 
Agreement is signed.
•Engineering Initial Application Fee (excluding R1 Building Permits)
o Low Density (1 - 11 storeys)
o Medium Density (12 - 29 storeys)
o High Density (30+ storeys)
•Latecomer Administration Fee
Discussion Key Changes and New Regulations
Implementation3
Implementation
1. Update the review and approval 
process for frontage upgrades. 
realize the potential efficiencies and 
improvements 
2. Align frontage review with the 
recently updated planning approval 
processes
•rezoning 
•development permit applications.
3. Develop Latecomer Agreement Policy
4. Communication3

Discussion Implementation

---

Public excluded in accordance with the Community Charter to consider matters concerning: 90(1)(k) negotiations and related discussions respecting the proposed provision of a municipal service that are at their preliminary stages and that, in the view of council, could reasonably be expected to harm the interests of the municipality if they were held in public.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Works and Services Bylaw Would Set New Development Infrastructure Rules
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to consider a new Works and Services Bylaw that would replace the city's 1971 Subdivision and Development Control Bylaw. The proposed bylaw would set clear, standardized rules for what infrastructure upgrades — such as water, sewer, roads, sidewalks, bike lanes, street trees, and lighting — developers must provide when building or subdividing land, depending on where in the city the project is located.
briefs.action_required: Review the proposed servicing requirements for your area — new upfront engineering application fees may apply to your next project.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Works and Services Bylaw — Committee of the Whole presentation date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Agriculture

briefs.title: Council to Consider Closed-Door Talks on Proposed New Municipal Service
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to hold a closed (private, members-only) session on April 27, 2026 to discuss early-stage negotiations about a possible new city service. Under BC's Community Charter — the law that governs how municipalities operate — Council is allowed to meet privately when talks are still in early stages and making them public could hurt the city's position.
briefs.action_required: Watch for future public announcements about this new city service, as details may affect your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council closed-session meeting on proposed municipal service
